---
title: "Multiple Linear Regression"
# subtitle: "More on classes"
author: |
    Khiem Nguyen 
    <br>Lecturer in Multiscale Materials
    <br><khiem.nguyen@glasgow.ac.uk>
author-meta: "Lecturer in Multiscale Materials"
format: 
    revealjs:
        # embed-resources: true
        theme: simple   # other themes: beige, blood, dark, dracula, league, moon, night, serif, simple, sky, solarized
        mouse-wheel: true
        highlight-style: github
        slide-number: true
        title-slide-style: pandoc
        echo: true
        code-overflow: wrap
        code-line-numbers: true
        code-block-bg: false          # <-- remove white background in code blocks
        # code-block-border-left: false # optional: remove left border
        # highlight-style: monokai      # good for dark images
        toc: true
        toc-depth: 1
        toc-title: Presentation Outline
        chalkboard: true
        css: simple.css
---

## From Simple to Multiple

In Multiple Linear Regression, we predict a target $y$ based on $d$ different features $(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d)$.

The prediction for a single observation is:
$$\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \dots + w_d x_d + b$$

We can simplify this using the **dot product**:
$$\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$

Where $\mathbf{w}$ and $\mathbf{x}$ are vectors in $\mathbb{R}^d$.

---

## The Matrix Formulation

For a dataset with $n$ observations, we stack the inputs into a matrix $\mathbf{X}$ of shape $(n \times d)$.

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w} + b\mathbf{1}$$

- $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$: Feature Matrix
- $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$: Weight Vector
- $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$: Prediction Vector
- $b$: Scalar Bias

---

## Loss Function (MSE)

The objective is to minimize the Mean Squared Error, expressed in vector notation:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{n} \|\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}\|^2_2$$

Expanding this:
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b - y^{(i)})^2$$

---

## The Gradient Vector

Because $\mathbf{w}$ is a vector, its gradient $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L}$ is also a vector of partial derivatives:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_1} \\ \vdots \\ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_d} \end{bmatrix}$$

Applying the chain rule to the matrix form:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = \frac{2}{n} \mathbf{X}^\top (\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w} + b - \mathbf{y})$$

---

## Gradient for Bias

The bias $b$ is a scalar, so its gradient is the average sum of all individual errors:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})$$

In vectorized notation:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = \frac{2}{n} \mathbf{1}^\top (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y})$$

---

## Multi-dimensional Update Rule

We update all weights simultaneously using the gradient vector and a learning rate $\alpha$:

$$\mathbf{w}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{w}_{\text{old}} - \alpha \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L}$$
$$b_{\text{new}} = b_{\text{old}} - \alpha \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b}$$

This iterative process allows the model to "learn" the optimal hyperplane.

---

## Geometric Interpretation

While Simple Linear Regression fits a **line**, Multiple Linear Regression fits a **hyperplane** in a $(d+1)$ dimensional space.

- $d=1$: A Line
- $d=2$: A Plane
- $d \ge 3$: A Hyperplane
